items.q: How does a harmonic minor scale differ from a natural minor?
items.correct: The seventh degree is raised by a semitone (creating a leading note).
items.misconception: All notes are raised by a semitone.
items.why: Students think the whole scale changes. Only the 7TH is altered to create a leading note, which strengthens cadences in minor keys - producing the characteristic augmented second between 6th and 7th.

items.q: What does modulation mean in music?
items.correct: Changing from one key to another within a piece.
items.why: Students conflate modulation with any musical change. Specifically, modulation is a KEY CHANGE, usually established by a perfect cadence in the new key - a structural device.

items.q: How does D Dorian differ from D minor?
items.correct: Dorian has a raised 6th (B natural) compared to natural minor.
items.random_wrong: Dorian has no sharps or flats at all.
items.why: Students treat modes as variants of major/minor. Dorian on D uses only white keys (like C major, but starting on D), giving a raised 6th compared to D natural minor - producing the modal flavour.
It's Diatonic. “Chromatic” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
It's Plucked. “Bowed” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A dominant pedal anchors how many beats per bar (in 4/4) when sustained throughout?
items.why: Exact answer: 4. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
